responseMessageContents.text:
The user scanned a QR code from api.qrserver.com that's causing an "Something went wrong" crash in Expo Go. The QR likely encodes an exp:// URL pointing to a tunnel that's no longer active, since the Metro server in the sandbox isn't running anymore.

I'm considering a few paths forward: publishing via EAS Update (though EAS isn't available in this environment), or trying to run Metro with a tunnel using ngrok or Expo's tunnel feature. The sandbox has internet access, so tunneling could work in theory, but the session is ephemeral — the server would die when the session ends.

Let me first decode the QR code from the image to see what URL it's pointing to, then determine if I can start a fresh Expo tunnel and generate a new QR for the user.

input.command:
cd /tmp && curl -s -o qr1.jpg "https://r2-pub.rork.com/attachments/uh7je3vvvvvg00epowqae.jpg" && python3 -c "
try:
    from PIL import Image
    print('PIL ok')
except Exception as e:
    print('no PIL', e)
" ; which zbarimg || pip list 2>/dev/null | rg -i "zbar|pyzbar|qr" || echo "no qr tools"

responseMessageContents.text: I need to decode the QR code to verify what URL is being scanned. I could install pyzbar, but that requires libzbar as a dependency. A simpler approach would be to use an online QR decoder API like the one at qrserver.com, which accepts file uploads and returns the decoded data without needing local libraries.

The QR code was pointing to a dead localtunnel URL from a previous sandbox session, which is why Expo Go couldn't connect. I need to start Metro with a fresh tunnel and generate a new QR code for the user, then verify the tunnel is actually reachable.

I'll start by running `bunx expo start --tunnel` to let Expo handle the tunnel setup automatically, wait for it to output the exp:// URL, curl the manifest endpoint to confirm it's responding, then generate a fresh QR code and share both the URL and the QR link with the user.The QR code points to a dead dev tunnel from a previous session—that's why Expo Go can't connect. I'll start a fresh tunnel now.

input.command: pkill -f "expo start" 2>/dev/null; pkill -f "localtunnel\|lt --port" 2>/dev/null; sleep 1; cd /home/user/rork-app/expo && (EXPO_NO_TELEMETRY=1 CI=0 nohup bunx expo start --port 8081 > /tmp/metro.log 2>&1 &) && echo "metro starting"

responseMessageContents.text: I'm debugging a bash script where the exit code 128 is appearing—likely from the backgrounded process in a subshell, since the pkill commands and sleep should return standard codes.

responseMessageContents.text:
The tunnel is exposing the manifest with a launchAsset URL pointing to port 8081, but loca.lt only supports ports 80 and 443, so Expo Go will fail when trying to fetch the bundle from that non-existent port. The issue is that the manifest constructs the bundle URL using the Host header combined with Metro's port, which doesn't work through the tunnel's constraints.

I need to use the `EXPO_PACKAGER_PROXY_URL` environment variable to override the bundle URL in the manifest — this tells Metro to report the bundle as being hosted at the tunnel URL without the port. Since the tunnel subdomain changes on each restart, I should request a fixed subdomain from localtunnel and then restart Metro with the proxy URL set to that stable tunnel address.

Once that's in place, I can verify the manifest shows the correct bundle URL, trigger a bundle build (which will take a while on first request), and then generate a QR code pointing to the tunnel URL for the user to scan.I found the issue — the manifest is pointing to port 8081 on the tunnel, but only port 443 exists. I'll restart Metro with the correct proxy URL to fix it.

input.command: pkill -f "localtunnel" ; pkill -f "expo start" ; sleep 2; cd /home/user/rork-app/expo && rm -f /tmp/lt.log && (setsid nohup bunx localtunnel --port 8081 --subdomain ivx26-app </dev/null > /tmp/lt.log 2>&1 &) ; sleep 10; cat /tmp/lt.log

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "pkill -f \"localtunnel\" ; pkill -f \"expo start\" ; sleep 2; cd /home/user/rork-app/expo && rm -f /tmp/lt.log && (setsid nohup bunx localtunnel --port 8081 --subdomain ivx26-app </dev/null > /tmp/lt.log 2>&1 &) ; sleep 10; cat /tmp/lt.log"}

responseMessageContents.text:
The pkill command is returning exit code 128 and terminating the entire process chain, possibly because the pattern is matching the parent shell itself rather than just the target process. I should switch to using separate commands with semicolons to avoid this issue, though I realize I already tried that approach. The real problem might be that patterns like "localtunnel" are too broad and matching my own command invocations instead of just the background processes.

I'll use a more specific pattern with character escaping, like `pkill -f "loca[l]tunnel"`, to avoid matching my own shell commands while still targeting the right processes.

input.command: pkill -f "loca[l]tunnel"; pkill -f "expo [s]tart"; sleep 2; ps aux | rg "8081|loca" | rg -v rg | head -3; echo done

output.stdout:
user         485  0.0  2.5 12330156 209788 ?     Sl   15:35   0:01 node /usr/local/bin/rork-agent start --port 19999
user         497  1.1  8.4 13333316 688616 ?     Sl   15:35   2:36 /usr/local/bin/node /usr/local/bin/rork-agent warm --port 19999
root         513  0.0  0.0  11716  4640 ?        S<   15:35   0:00 socat -d -d -d TCP4-LISTEN:19999,bind=169.254.0.21,reuseaddr,fork TCP4:localhost:19999
done

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "pkill -f \"loca[l]tunnel\"; pkill -f \"expo [s]tart\"; sleep 2; ps aux | rg \"8081|loca\" | rg -v rg | head -3; echo done"}

responseMessageContents.text: Now I'm killing the old processes and setting up localtunnel with a fixed subdomain to get a stable URL, then launching Metro with the packager proxy pointing to that tunnel URL.

responseMessageContents.text: The bundle built successfully in about 24 seconds with over 4000 modules. Now I'm verifying the bundle is accessible through the tunnel and creating a QR code pointing to the exp://ivx26-app.loca.lt endpoint for the user.Bundle compiled successfully with 4,226 modules. Now I'll verify it's accessible through the public tunnel and generate your QR code.

The tunnel download completed successfully despite hitting the timeout—it was pulling at about 330KB/s, so the full bundle will take around two minutes on the phone, which is manageable since Expo Go displays a progress indicator. Now I'll generate the QR code pointing to exp://ivx26-app.loca.lt and share that with the user.

The tunnel is serving the bundle (HTTP 200 — my test cut at 55s only due to my own timeout; the download was progressing fine at ~18MB). Everything is live. Here's your fresh connection:

---

## Why it wasn't working

The QR code you scanned points to **`exp://easy-humans-sing.loca.lt`** — a dev tunnel from a previous session that no longer exists. Expo Go reached a dead address → "Something went wrong."

I also found and fixed a second, deeper bug: the old tunnel's manifest told Expo Go to download the app bundle from port `:8081` on the tunnel domain — a port the tunnel never exposes. So even a live tunnel would have failed. The new server is configured so all URLs go through the tunnel correctly (verified: manifest HTTP 200, bundle HTTP 200, 4,226 modules compiled).

## Scan this NEW QR code

Open this link on any screen and scan it with Expo Go:

**https://api.qrserver.com/v1/create-qr-code/?size=400x400&data=exp%3A%2F%2Fivx26-app.loca.lt**

Or in Expo Go, tap **"Enter URL manually"** and type:

```
exp://ivx26-app.loca.lt
```

## What to expect

1. Before scanning: long-press the old IVX project in Expo Go → remove it, then force-close and reopen Expo Go
2. After scanning, you'll see a **progress bar for 1–3 minutes** — the dev bundle is ~40 MB going through a tunnel. Don't close it; let it finish
3. The IVX landing screen should appear

One honest caveat: this tunnel lives inside my working session, so it works **right now** — test it as soon as you can. If the session recycles later, the address dies again and I'll spin up a fresh one. Send me a screenshot of what you see either way.
